# The Mental Model

Chapter 1 promised that this chapter draws the full mental
model, and that is all it does. Nothing here is a trick or a
technique. It is a small set of ideas, maybe an afternoon's
worth, that the rest of the book stands on: what the shell
actually is, what happens when you press return, where your
files live, how a command gets found, and how a running
command talks back to you. Skip it and the later chapters
still work, but they work like incantations. Read it and they
become predictable, which is the difference between copying
commands and using a tool.

The payoff for a researcher is specific. When a script that
works on your laptop fails on a server, the failure is almost
always one of this chapter's five nouns: the process, the
working directory, the environment, the PATH, or the exit
code. The error message will assume you know what those words
mean. After this chapter you will.

A note on where the output comes from. From here on, every
command this book shows is really run, and its output is
pasted from a saved transcript, never invented. The portable
GNU/Linux demonstrations in this chapter ran in the book's
Linux sandbox (Ubuntu 22.04, GNU bash 5.1, GNU coreutils
8.32; the `ch02-*` transcript files), some of them inside the
asset-pricing project this book threads throughout, and some
in a throwaway `/tmp` directory. The macOS side, zsh, the BSD
tools, and Homebrew's PATH, was captured on the author's Mac
(macOS 26.5.1, zsh 5.9; `transcripts/ch02-*-mac.txt`,
produced by `capture-ch02-mac.sh`) and is quoted where the
platforms genuinely differ. One deliberate mask: in Mac
output, the account segment of home paths reads
`/Users/[account]`, a documented scrub for this public book.
The brackets are a placeholder, not something a machine
prints: run the same commands yourself and that segment
shows your own account name. Everything else in every block
is verbatim from its transcript. The one figure in this chapter is an authored
diagram, not captured output.

## Terminal, shell, operating system {.tier-beginner}

Three different programs cooperate every time you type a
command, and the words for them are used interchangeably in
casual speech, which is where a lot of early confusion comes
from. This book keeps them apart.

The **terminal** is the window: an ordinary application
window on your screen, lowercase, no relation to the Windows
operating system. It draws text, and it sends what you type
onward. It runs no commands itself; Terminal on
a Mac, the WSL2 window on Windows, and the console you see
after SSH-ing to a server are all just this thin layer.
The **shell** is the program running inside that window, and
it is the actual subject of this book. It prints the prompt,
reads the line you typed, expands any variables and wildcard
patterns in it, works out which program you are asking for,
and asks the operating system to run that program. bash and
zsh are the two shells this book cares about; they are close
relatives, and Chapter 3 will sort out which one you have and
why, along with how to open a terminal on each platform and
why Windows readers should work inside WSL2 rather than
PowerShell. The **operating system kernel** is the layer underneath
both: the only thing that actually creates processes, reads
and writes the disk, and moves bytes on the network. The
shell is not the machine's engine. It is the machine's
dispatcher, and everything it dispatches runs at the kernel's
pleasure.

![The layers between a keystroke and a result. The terminal
passes your line to the shell; the shell finds the program
and asks the kernel to run it; the kernel starts the process;
output flows back up to the terminal while the exit code
reports back to the
shell.](../assets/figures/ch02/fig-ch02-layers){#fig-ch02-layers
fig-alt="A vertical diagram of five boxes: You, Terminal,
Shell, Operating system kernel, and a running process. Arrows
flow downward from typing to the running process; a green
arrow returns stdout and stderr from the process to the
terminal, and a dashed amber arrow returns the exit code from
the process to the shell." width="4.6in" fig-pos="!tbp"}

Keep one sentence from this section if you keep nothing else:
**the shell runs commands.** That is its whole job, and the
division of labor around it is the map of your toolkit. Git
versions files; that discipline is the companion Git book's
entire subject. Make decides *which* commands need to run and
in what order, then hands them to the shell (Chapter 11). uv
and renv, which the Git book introduces and Chapter 12 will
teach operationally, reproduce the package environment your
commands run inside. Tools like `rsync`, run from the shell,
will move your data between machines in Chapter 13. And the
AI agents of Chapter 16 decide which commands to run and hand
them to this same shell. Every one of those tools is a
customer of the same narrow service, and when something
breaks, the first diagnostic question is always: which layer
failed, the tool, the shell that launched it, or the system
underneath?

\FloatBarrier

## One tree of files {.tier-beginner}

Everything the shell touches lives in a single tree of
directories that starts at the **root**, written `/`. There
is no C: drive and no separate disk letters; every disk,
folder, and file hangs somewhere off that one root. A
**path** names a location in the tree, and it comes in two
forms. An **absolute path** starts with `/` and spells out
the location from the root; it means the same thing no matter
where it is used. A **relative path** starts anywhere else
and is interpreted relative to the **working directory**, the
place in the tree a program is currently standing. (Windows
proper is the exception that uses drive letters, but it is
not this book's platform: inside WSL2 you work in exactly
this single Unix tree, with the Windows `C:` drive mounted
into it at `/mnt/c`, and Chapter 3 will return to what that
means in practice.)

Every running program has a working directory, and `pwd`
prints your shell's. This is also the book's first command
block, so recall Chapter 1's reading rule: type only what
follows the `$`, and expect the unprefixed lines back as
output. In a scratch directory in the sandbox,
holding one empty file `notes.txt`, the same file answers to
both kinds of name:

```bash
$ pwd
/tmp/ch02-fs/project
$ ls /tmp/ch02-fs/project/notes.txt
/tmp/ch02-fs/project/notes.txt
$ ls notes.txt
notes.txt
```

The two `ls` calls name the identical file; the second is
shorter only because the shell is already standing in
`/tmp/ch02-fs/project`. This is why an instruction like "run
`ls data/raw`" carries a silent precondition: it works from
the project's top directory and fails from anywhere else,
because the relative path is resolved against wherever you
happen to be standing. When a later chapter's command fails
with "No such file or directory" on a file you can see in a
window, the working directory is the first suspect. Moving
around the tree, and the file operations that go with it, is
Chapter 4's subject; here you only need the model.

Two locations get shorthand names. Your **home directory** is
where your login starts and where your own files live; the
shell abbreviates it `~`. On the author's Mac, home is under
`/Users`, and the top of the tree looks like this:

```bash
$ echo $HOME
/Users/[account]
$ ls /
Applications
Library
System
Users
Volumes
bin
cores
dev
etc
home
opt
private
sbin
tmp
usr
var
```

Most of those are system directories you will rarely touch by
hand. A handful are worth recognizing, and they mean the same
thing across Unix systems. Here is the top of a Linux server's
tree, annotated (macOS is the same shape, with your home under
`/Users` rather than `/home`):

```text
/            the root of everything (one tree)
|-- home/    user home directories (your ~)
|-- usr/     installed programs and libraries
|-- etc/     system-wide configuration files
|-- var/     variable data: logs and caches
|-- tmp/     temporary files, cleared on reboot
`-- opt/     optional add-on software
```

The shape is the same everywhere: one rooted tree, your
territory a few levels down inside your home. The project
layout discipline for what goes where *inside* your territory
is the companion Git book's canonical-layout argument;
Chapter 5 will build that layout from the shell.

## A command becomes a process {.tier-beginner}

When the shell runs a program, the kernel creates a
**process**: one running instance of that program, with its
own identity and its own copy of everything it needs. The
identity is a number, the **process ID** or PID. The shell
itself is a process; the special variable `$$` holds its PID,
and `ps` will report on it:

```bash
$ echo $$
5
$ ps -o pid,ppid,comm -p $$
    PID    PPID COMMAND
      5       2 bash
```

The shell here is PID 5, and the PPID column names its
**parent**, the process that started it. (PIDs this small are
a quirk of the book's sandbox, which numbers its processes
from scratch; on your laptop expect a much larger number.)
Every process has a parent, so the system is a family tree: the
terminal starts a shell, the shell starts your commands, and
each command is a **child process** of the shell. Ask a child
shell for *its* PID and you get a different number each time,
because each run is a fresh process:

```bash
$ bash -c 'echo $$'
7
$ bash -c 'echo $$'
8
```

`bash -c` starts a new bash (a child), hands it the quoted
command, and waits for it to finish. Same program, two runs,
two processes, two PIDs: a process is one *run*, not one
program. That distinction is why you can run two copies of
the same script side by side, and it is what makes the
remote-compute chapters possible at all: a long job is just a
process that must be kept alive, which is exactly what tmux
will do for you in Chapter 14. Managing processes you have
started, pausing them, backgrounding them, stopping them, is
Chapter 9's subject.

## Why `cd` is not a program {.tier-advanced}

Most commands are separate programs on disk. A few are not,
and the reason is the process model from the last section.
`type` asks the shell how it would interpret a name:

```bash
$ type cd
cd is a shell builtin
$ type ls
ls is /usr/bin/ls
```

`ls` is an external program, a file the kernel runs as a new
child process. `cd` is a **builtin**, a command the shell
performs itself, in its own process, without starting a
child. It has to be. A child process gets its own *copy* of
the working directory and the environment, and nothing a
child does to its copies can reach back and change the
parent. If `cd` were an external program, it would
dutifully change its own working directory and then exit,
leaving your shell exactly where it started. Any command
whose whole point is to change *the shell's own state*
(`cd`, `export` from the next section, `set` and friends)
must therefore be a builtin.

A name can be both. `echo` is a builtin in bash *and* a
program on disk, and `type -a` lists every interpretation in
the order they would be tried:

```bash
$ type -a echo
echo is a shell builtin
echo is /usr/bin/echo
echo is /bin/echo
```

The shell uses its builtin first. Which of several same-named
files would win, and how the shell finds files at all, is the
PATH question, two sections ahead. The practical takeaway is
diagnostic: when a command behaves differently from what its
manual says, `type` tells you whether you are even running
the thing whose manual you read.

## The environment: what a process inherits {.tier-beginner}

Besides a working directory, every process carries a set of
named strings called **environment variables**: `HOME` is
your home directory, `PATH` is the next section's subject,
`LANG` picks a language, and so on. By convention the names
are uppercase. The `env` command lists your shell's
environment; even in this book's spartan sandbox there are a
few dozen entries:

```bash
$ env | wc -l
38
```

(The `| wc -l` counts the lines instead of printing them;
the pipe machinery is Chapter 6's subject.) The environment
matters because of how it moves: when the shell starts a
child process, the child inherits a **copy** of the shell's
environment. That is the mechanism by which your settings
reach your programs. It is also a one-way street, parent to
child, at the moment the child starts. Changes made in the
child never propagate back up, and changes made in the parent
afterward never reach a child already running.

There is one catch, and it bites researchers constantly. A
variable you assign in the shell is, by default, a private
**shell variable**, not part of the environment at all. The
child cannot see it until you `export` it:

```bash
$ DATASET=panel_v2
$ bash -c 'echo "child sees: ${DATASET:-unset}"'
child sees: unset
$ export DATASET
$ bash -c 'echo "child sees: ${DATASET:-unset}"'
child sees: panel_v2
```

The `${DATASET:-unset}` form prints the variable's value, or
the word `unset` when there is none, so the child's view is
visible either way. Before the `export`, the child shell,
and it could just as well be your Python or R process, has
no idea `DATASET` exists.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
"I set the variable, but my script does not see it" is the
classic environment bug, and the cause is almost always one
of two things: the variable was assigned but never exported,
as above, or it was exported in one shell and the script ran
in a different one (a new terminal window, a job on a
server), which inherited nothing from the first. A shell's
exported variables die with it unless a startup file
recreates them; wiring settings so they exist in every new
shell, and understanding why a script run by `cron` or over
SSH sees a much smaller environment than your terminal does,
is Chapter 17's subject. When in doubt, make the process
print its own view, as the `bash -c` probe above does; what
*it* sees is the only thing that counts.
:::

The environment is also quietly a reproducibility surface:
variables like `LC_ALL` and `TZ` change how tools sort text
and parse dates, which can change results between machines.
Chapter 10 will pin them in scripts. And because environment
variables are so convenient for configuration, they are a
favorite place to put secrets like API keys, which makes them
a leak surface too; keeping keys out of the environment and
your shell history is part of Chapter 9's job, alongside the
Git book's rules for keeping them out of repositories.

## How the shell finds a command: `PATH` {.tier-beginner}

When you type `python3`, the shell has to turn that word into
a file to execute, and it does not search the whole disk. It
walks one environment variable, `PATH`, a colon-separated
list of directories, left to right, and runs the **first**
match it finds. The sandbox's PATH is the stock server
layout:

```bash
$ echo $PATH
/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin
$ command -v python3
/usr/bin/python3
```

`command -v` answers the only question that matters when
something misbehaves: *which file will actually run when I
type this name?* Its more talkative cousin `type -a` shows
every candidate in search order:

```bash
$ type -a sort
sort is /usr/bin/sort
sort is /bin/sort
```

Two entries because both `/usr/bin` and `/bin` are on the
PATH and Ubuntu makes them point at the same files; the first
one listed wins. First-match-wins is the rule to internalize,
because everything interesting about PATH follows from it.
"Command not found" means no directory on the PATH contains
that name, not that the tool is absent from the machine; a
freshly installed tool that "does not work" usually landed in
a directory the PATH does not list; and two installed
versions of the same tool means the earlier directory
silently decides which one you get. First-match-wins is also
what makes an *attacker-controlled* early directory
dangerous, the PATH-hijacking risk that Chapter 6 pairs with
`curl | sh` and Chapter 16 revisits for AI agents. Where
PATH itself gets set, and how to change yours safely, is
startup-file territory: Chapter 3 will set you up and Chapter
17 will make it stick.

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
`command -v python3` is the one-line answer to a question
that has burned real papers: *which Python just produced my
results?* A Mac with Homebrew, a course-installed Anaconda,
and a project virtual environment can easily carry three or
four interpreters at once, each with different package
versions, and PATH order alone decides which one the word
`python3` means today. A result that depends on "whichever
interpreter happened to be first on PATH" is not reproducible
in any useful sense. The cure is to make the choice explicit
and portable: project environments (uv for Python, renv for
R), which put a known interpreter first on the PATH while a
project is active and pin its packages. The companion Git
book introduces those tools; Chapter 12 will drive them from
the shell. Until then, the habit is the diagnostic: when a
result surprises you, ask `command -v` before you ask
anything else.
:::

::: {.callout-note .divergence title="DIVERGENCE"}
On a Mac the same machinery runs with two twists, both real,
neither deep. First, Homebrew, the standard macOS package
manager, installs everything under `/opt/homebrew` on Apple
Silicon (Homebrew's documented default prefix; Intel Macs
used `/usr/local`), and its setup puts `/opt/homebrew/bin` at
the *front* of your PATH. On the author's Mac the login PATH
begins `/opt/homebrew/bin:/opt/homebrew/sbin:/usr/local/bin:`
and only then reaches the system's `/usr/bin` and `/bin`
(`transcripts/ch02-divergence-mac.txt`; the line's personal
tail of user tool directories is trimmed there, marked
`[...]`). That
front position is the whole point, Homebrew's newer tools
must beat the older system copies, but it means macOS
answers to "which `sort` runs?" depend on what you have
brewed. Second, the popular `which` command is not one thing
across platforms: on Linux it is an external program that
knows nothing about builtins or aliases, while in zsh it is
itself a builtin with fuller knowledge. Asked about `which`,
each platform confesses:

```bash
$ type -a which
which is /usr/bin/which
which is /bin/which
```

on the sandbox's bash, but on the Mac's zsh:

```bash
$ zsh -c 'type -a which'
which is a shell builtin
which is /usr/bin/which
```

The portable habit: use `command -v` (POSIX-specified,
works everywhere) or your shell's `type`, and treat `which`
as the one to distrust in instructions you find online.
:::

## Three streams, in the abstract {.tier-beginner}

Every process is born with three communication channels
already open, and the names recur in every error message and
every later chapter, so meet them here. **Standard input**
(stdin) is where a process reads from; at an interactive
prompt it is your keyboard. **Standard output** (stdout) is
where it writes its results. **Standard error** (stderr) is
where it writes complaints and progress reports. Results and
complaints get separate channels on purpose, so that one can
be saved or passed onward while the other still reaches you.

Seeing the seam takes a command that writes to both streams
at once, and that needs real files, so this is also where the
running example first appears on the page. The asset-pricing
study Chapter 1 sketched lives in the book's repository under
`sandbox/asset-pricing/`: a seeded Python script in its
`scripts/` folder generates the two raw inputs,
`data/raw/factors.csv` (180 months of simulated factor
returns) and `data/raw/firm_panel.csv` (a panel of 300 firms
over those months), and everything the book later does with
them amounts to one long-short portfolio, one factor
regression, one table, and one figure in a short rendered
report. The data files are not stored in the repository;
because the generator is seeded, running it recreates them
identically, which is itself a reproducibility lesson.
That is all the finance you need: Chapter 5 will tour the
project directory piece by piece, and Chapter 11 rebuilds
the entire study with one command. Here the files are
simply realistic material to point commands at.

On a plain terminal you cannot *see* the seam, because both
output streams land on the same screen. But it is there. Ask
`wc -l` to count lines in one file that exists and one that
does not, inside the asset-pricing project:

```bash
$ wc -l data/raw/factors.csv missing.csv
  181 data/raw/factors.csv
wc: missing.csv: No such file or directory
  181 total
```

Three lines, two channels: the count and the total went to
stdout, the complaint went to stderr, and the terminal
interleaved them. (`wc` still prints a total for what it
*could* read, which is exactly the kind of polite
partial-success that hides missing inputs from you; the exit
code in the next section is the honest witness.) Keeping the
two output streams apart is what will let you, in Chapter 6,
save clean results to a file while errors still print, or
chain commands into pipelines without the diagnostics
polluting the data. That chapter owns all the mechanics of
re-pointing these streams; this section only needs you to
know the three channels exist, are distinct, and are wired
to every process the kernel starts.

## Exit codes: how a command reports back {.tier-beginner}

Output is for you; the **exit code** is for the machinery.
Every process, when it ends, hands the kernel one small
integer, and the shell delivers it to the special variable
`$?`. The convention is universal: **zero means success,
anything else means failure.** Still inside the project:

```bash
$ ls data/raw
factors.csv
firm_panel.csv
$ echo $?
0
$ ls nope_dir
ls: cannot access 'nope_dir': No such file or directory
$ echo $?
2
```

The successful listing reported 0; the failed one reported 2,
which GNU `ls` documents as "serious trouble" (1 would be
minor trouble, such as an unreadable subdirectory). Beyond
zero-versus-nonzero, the specific numbers are each tool's
private vocabulary, documented in its manual; do not assume
one tool's 2 means another's.

Exit codes look redundant next to error messages, but they
are the only signal the *machinery* can act on. A human reads
"No such file or directory"; a Makefile, a script, or an AI
agent reads `$?`. Everything this book builds later stands on
this convention: Make stops a build when a recipe exits
nonzero (Chapter 11), scripts will stop on the first failure
via `set -e` (Chapter 10), and pipelines have a whole trap of
their own with `pipefail`, which Chapter 6 demonstrates. A
tool that lies about its exit code, and some do, breaks every
one of those layers at once, which is why Chapter 10 will
teach you to check what your own scripts report.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
`$?` holds the exit code of the **most recent** command,
full stop, and reading it is not a free action if anything
runs in between. Continue the session above:

```bash
$ echo $?
0
```

The failure is gone. This third `echo $?` reports on the
*second* one, which printed `2` successfully and thereby
reset the status to 0. Anything you run between a command
and your check of `$?`, another `echo`, an `ls` to look
around, overwrites the evidence. Check `$?` immediately, or
save it first (`rc=$?`) if you need it later. This chapter's
own capture process tripped exactly this trap on its first
attempt, an `echo` slipped between the failing command and
the check and reported a clean 0, so treat it as a when,
not an if. In scripts, `set -e` (Chapter 10) will remove
the need to babysit `$?` by hand.
:::

## Getting unstuck: `--help`, `man`, and `tldr` {.tier-beginner}

The mental model's last piece is knowing where the answers
live, because nobody memorizes options. There are three
levels, from quickest to deepest, and a researcher wants all
three within reach.

The first level is the tool itself. Nearly every GNU tool
answers `--help` with a usage summary on stdout:

```bash
$ ls --help | head -6
Usage: ls [OPTION]... [FILE]...
List information about the FILEs (the current directory by default).
Sort entries alphabetically if none of -cftuvSUX nor --sort is specified.

Mandatory arguments to long options are mandatory for short options too.
  -a, --all                  do not ignore entries starting with .
```

(The `| head -6` keeps the first six lines so the page fits
here; run it bare and you get the whole summary.) The second
level is the **manual page**: `man ls` opens the full
reference in a scrolling viewer, arrow keys to move, `q` to
quit. On the author's Mac, trimmed the same way for print:

```bash
$ man ls | col -b | head -12
LS(1)			    General Commands Manual			 LS(1)

NAME
     ls – list directory contents

SYNOPSIS
     ls [-@ABCFGHILOPRSTUWabcdefghiklmnopqrstuvwxy1%,] [--color=when]
	[-D format] [file ...]

DESCRIPTION
     For each operand that names a file of a type other than directory, ls
     displays its name as well as any requested, associated information.  For
```

(The `col -b` strips the pager's typesetting so the excerpt
prints cleanly; at your own prompt, plain `man ls` is all you
need.) The `(1)` after the name is the manual's section
number, section 1 being ordinary commands; you can ignore
sections until a tool and a config-file format share a name.
The man page is the authority for *your* system, which is
exactly its value on a platform split: this page is the BSD
`ls` that macOS actually ships, not the GNU `ls` a random web
tutorial assumes.

The third level is `tldr`, a community-maintained collection
of worked examples, one screenful per command, showing the
half-dozen invocations people actually use. It is a separate
install (Chapter 3 will handle it; the client captured here
is tealdeer 1.8.1, current as of 2026-07-01):

```bash
$ tldr tar | head -14

  Archiving utility.
  Often combined with a compression method, such as `gzip` or `bzip2`.
  More information: <https://www.gnu.org/software/tar/manual/tar.html>.

  [c]reate an archive and write it to a [f]ile:

      tar cf path/to/target.tar path/to/file1 path/to/file2 ...

  [c]reate a g[z]ipped archive and write it to a [f]ile:

      tar czf path/to/target.tar.gz path/to/file1 path/to/file2 ...

  [c]reate a g[z]ipped (compressed) archive from a directory using relative paths:
```

Examples first, prose never: for the daily "what was that
flag again," it is usually the fastest of the three. Its
pages are community-written simplifications, though, so when
precision matters, or `tldr` is not installed on the server
you just landed on, the man page remains the authority.

Two honest caveats complete the toolkit. First, the three
levels plus `type`/`command -v` from earlier compose into a
diagnostic ladder: *what will run* (`command -v`), *what can
it do* (`--help`), *what exactly does this option mean*
(`man`), *how do people actually use it* (`tldr`). Second,
do not assume the ladder is fully present everywhere:

```bash
$ man grep
No manual entry for grep
```

That is this book's own sandbox, a minimized Ubuntu image
that ships a real `man` command but strips most of the pages
to save space, as slimmed-down server and container images
often do. The `--help` level, living inside the tools
themselves, survives everywhere the tool does.

::: {.callout-note .divergence title="DIVERGENCE"}
`--help` itself is a GNU habit, not a law of nature. The BSD
tools that macOS ships mostly do not implement it; they
answer with an error and a terse usage line:

```bash
$ /bin/ls --help; echo "exit=$?"
ls: unrecognized option `--help'
usage: ls [-@ABCFGHILOPRSTUWXabcdefghiklmnopqrstuvwxy1%,] [--color=when] [-D format] [file ...]
exit=1
```

Note the exit code: asking a BSD tool for help is itself a
failing command. The usage line it grudgingly prints is
real information, but for anything more on a Mac you go
straight to `man`. The same command name, `ls`, thus carries
different flags, different help behavior, and different
documentation on the two platforms this book spans, which is
the BSD/GNU divergence Chapter 1 promised in miniature. When
a web tutorial's flag "does not exist" on your Mac, suspect
the platform before you suspect yourself.
:::

## Try it yourself {.unnumbered}

These run anywhere and destroy nothing. Use the asset-pricing
project from your copy of the book's repository if you have
it; only exercises 1 and 4 need it.

1. Stand in the project's top directory and run `pwd`. Write
   down the absolute path of `data/raw/factors.csv` by
   combining the two, then verify with `ls` that both the
   absolute path and the relative path name the same file.
   Then `cd` to your home directory and confirm the relative
   form now fails while the absolute form still works.

2. Run `echo $$`, then `bash -c 'echo $$'` three times. Say
   in one sentence why the first number repeats and the
   other three all differ. Then run `type cd` and explain,
   in terms of parents and children, why `cd` could not do
   its job as an external program.

3. Ask `command -v python3` and `type -a python3` on your
   machine. Is the interpreter that answers the one you
   thought you were using? If you have R, repeat for `R`.
   Keep the answer somewhere; when Chapter 12 introduces
   project environments, rerun both and watch the answer
   change while a project is active.

4. In the project, rerun this chapter's failing count,
   `wc -l data/raw/factors.csv missing.csv`, and check `$?`
   immediately. Then check `$?` again and explain the value
   you got the second time. Finally, rerun the count and put
   an `ls` between the command and your first check; predict
   what `$?` will say before you look.

5. Reproduce the inheritance experiment with your own
   variable name: assign without `export` and probe with
   `bash -c`, then export and probe again. Then open a *new*
   terminal window and probe once more, and explain what the
   result tells you about where exported variables live and
   when they die.

6. Pick a command you half-know, `sort` is a good choice,
   and climb the ladder on your platform: `sort --help` (or
   `man sort` first on a Mac), then the man page, then
   `tldr sort` if you have it. Note one flag you did not
   know. If you are on a Mac, compare `man sort`'s header
   with the web page for GNU sort and confirm they document
   different implementations.
